FT_IC1.evidence: Longitudinal data from U.S. ABCD Study (21 sites, ages 9-10 baseline); analyzed data collected at year 2 and year 4 follow-ups with MID task fMRI data

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed models examining VGA × time interactions on neural activation during MID task reward/loss processing, with cross-sectional and longitudinal hypothesis testing

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Computers in Human Behavior 165 (2025), pp. 108554

FT_IC2.evidence: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (6-item, operationalizing addictive gaming use) paired with neural reward/loss sensitivity (MID task beta-weights for nucleus accumbens, insula, ACC, amygdala). Neural processing is individual-trait-level outcome. VGA appears as IV (Model 1) and DV (Model 2) in separate analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential analyses performed: linear mixed models with interaction effects (VGA × Time, neural sensitivity × Time) on neural responses and addiction scores; results reported with 95% CI, p-values, and effect sizes

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported throughout: VGA × time interactions on loss contrast in left nucleus accumbens (b=0.106, p=.002), left insula (b=0.103, p=.002), right insula (b=0.111, p=.001), right ACC (b=0.111, p=.001); neural loss sensitivity × time predicting VGA in right amygdala (b=0.086, p=.003) and right ACC (b=0.088, p=.003)

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design with bidirectional analyses: VGA predicts neural loss sensitivity AND neural loss sensitivity predicts later VGA. Video game addiction analyzed as both IV (Model 1: predicting neural changes) and DV (Model 2: predicted by neural sensitivity). EC7 applies only to DV-only designs; bidirectional disqualifies it.
notes: Strong longitudinal evidence for bidirectional relationship between video game addiction and neural reward processing dysfunction in adolescence. SMA analyzed separately and showed no comparable effects.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cohort data obtained from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study Release 5.1; cross-sectional networks at T2 (2-year, ages 11-12) and T4 (4-year, ages 13-14) follow-ups; cross-lagged panel network analyses across 3,954 youth

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional network analyses identifying centrality indices and cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) modeling testing bidirectional relationships between screen use measures and developmental outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed full-length report published in Journal of Behavioral Addictions 14(2):724-737, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic screen use operationalized as screen-related functional impairment (continuous measure of severity) measured via self-report questionnaires capturing experiential and motivational aspects. Paired with individual-trait-level outcomes: psychopathology (anxiety/depression, somatic symptoms, social problems, attention/thought problems, rule-breaking, aggressive behavior), family conflict, peer involvement, school participation. Paper emphasizes problematic use as functional impairment construct distinct from raw screen time.

FT_IC3.evidence: Network analyses with inferential testing: cross-sectional networks computed using Extended Bayesian Information Criterion graphical lasso; cross-lagged panel network analyses with CLPN modeling testing associations between screen use measures at T2 and psychopathology/environment at T4

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: 'Problematic use exhibited high out-strength, which was associated with worsening psychopathologies and environmental conditions. Conversely, problematic use at T4 appeared less influenced by factors at T2.' Cross-lagged associations quantified via network edge strengths and centrality metrics.

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design with screen use (and problematic use) analyzed as predictor (IV) of psychopathology and environmental factors. Screen use is not exclusively DV in all analyses. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Emphasis on problematic screen use as functional impairment rather than raw time; network analysis reveals high centrality of problematic use in predicting worse developmental outcomes than self-reported screen time alone.

FT_IC1.evidence: Subsample of 3,687 early adolescents from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study; year 2 follow-up with Fitbit wearables (ages ~12) and year 3 follow-up (ages ~13) questionnaire data; subset wore wearables for up to 21 nights

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective investigation with structural equation modeling examining associations between sleep (objective via Fitbit; subjective sleep duration/timing), sensation-seeking traits, and mature screen usage at 1-year follow-up; secondary bidirectional analyses

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Sleep journal, 2025, doi: 10.1093/sleep/zsae234

FT_IC2.evidence: Outcome variable is mature screen usage (mature-rated video games and R-rated movies). According to v6 criteria, 'Content-rating variables (mature-rated games, R-rated movies) alone do NOT qualify; rating ≠ modality.' The paper does not measure smartphone use, social media use, or phone-specific activity. It measures content maturity/rating, not device or platform modality. No per-device breakdown of phone or social media use is provided in the analysis.

notes: Paper focuses on content maturity (R-rated, mature-rated) rather than smartphone/social media modality. While sleep is measured objectively and sensation-seeking is an individual-trait variable, the exposure is content rating, not SM/phone use. Fails FT-IC2; cascade stops.
